content:
5 min read
Just now

--

You know what it’s like to be a Gen‐Z American? Well, ChatGPT seems to. Although it must look from the outside in — it will never be conscious in my eyes; call me unsympathetic — generations after Z, also on the outside, would be at a loss to judge who knows us better if our respective accounts were laid side by side. Is it we ourselves, who can’t write for shit, or the LLMS, who seem to know all that makes us zoom without knowing what zooming’s like? Well, forget all you don’t know, because, for all you know, this account was written by the last generation (because we’re last in the alphabet and that’s how that works, right?) and not by an LLM, which means that you can later use it as the human side of the comparison. I’d advise you to, like, make sure you, like, double‐blind yourselves or something but
Transitioning like an ill‐oiled LLM, the defining feeling of my generation is probably consciousness itself. Specifically, consciousness overstuffed with itself. Ours is a consciousness aware of everything everywhere all at once and self‐aware: that was a borrowed phrase. It’s somehow sleepier the more wakeful it is; nowhere because everywhere. Seneca shmenica. It’s bird‐witted. Did I say self‐aware? Our memory is like a reverse black hole that blows everything out except what doesn’t clear the threshold, the event horizon of immediacy, that is, all things that aren’t happening now, and of those things, only those with a claim on our attention whose validity, which we judge for ourselves, we must therefore judge without appeal to any precedent whatever. Our consciousness is aware — well, would be aware if it was aware of anything before now — that it’s not even particularly distinctive (because didn’t Infinite Jest, which I am too short‐suffering to actually read (in all irony, and yes, this is a nested parenthetical), lend this sort of consciousness definition back in the ‘90s?). The prevailing symptom of this condition is a helpless, incessant sick‐and‐tiredness which negotiates, by turns, an unhappy marriage to hope and a happy marriage to despair through the mediation of distraction.
All the Zs who aren’t zzzing can call themselves a curious sort of Cassandra: a Cassandra without special insight, whom everyone agrees with, yet whose prophesied doom will probably kill us all anyway. Luddism toward AI hangs heavy in the air, not just as huffed out by my peers who are entering an ever‐narrowing labor market, but as sighed out resignedly by older generations. Even if we all grudgingly admit AI’s usefulness, everyone would sooner see it regulated and the paradise it promises temporarily lost than it come for their jobs or their lives. And yet it’s coming for the one certainly and the other potentially.
We lemmings are running off a cliff. Stet that shit ’cause some level of shittiness is optimal now. We’re like lemmings being, like — fuck — stampeded off a cliff by lawless cowboys — Musk, Altman, Zuckerberg, and so on down the rogues’ gallery. However strongly our little lemming hands may be linked together in opposition, we’re fucking lemmings. I hesitate to say that it’s the steeds themselves, riderless, who are doing the stampeding — steeds with a nightmarishly real instinct for survival and reproduction. I hesitate because, while that is more metaphorically apt, it would seem to excuse the people who loosed the chargers on humanity, people at whom I thrust my phallic middle finger and thereby magically refocus all the responsibility that they’ve successfully, systematically, diffused. Zuckerberg has already fucked my entire generation in the head. Should we really let him develop chatbots with the ability to hack our attachment systems, replacing human friendship especially among those of the neuro‐pliable next generation? The question is only rhetorical: of course nobody wants that, but it’s coming anyway. You know why? Also rhetorical. Our democracy’s not exactly Norwegian. Though we would all keep this incarnation of the jitters away from our children, he’s about to give our children his heebie‐jeebies hug, or, I guess, encourage his digital proxies to do so.
I concede — to you? myself? — that there is absolutely no point in bestowing fury on these pixels. Raise all the awareness that there is to raise in whom there is any awareness, so long as they’re all plebs like me, they can’t do anything about it. As Timothy Snyder says — I don’t read books; I just pull useful quotations from the ether — “The odd American idea that giving money to political campaigns is free speech means that the very rich have far more free speech, and so in effect far more voting power, than other citizens.”
I can only hope that the foregoing was — and the ensuing shall be — noisy and incoherent enough to reveal some intentionality behind the text. Why else would readers read me when they can read the lucid oracle? Even if the Chinese Room thought experiment is right that sense cannot be divorced from intention, I fear that what is merely sensical (the outputs of ChatGPT) already do and will increasingly seem to be more intentional than what is actually so (the works of human authors). Is there any forgery that cannot be made to seem more convincing than the original? If not, how can anyone prove their literary, ultimately their intellectual, authenticity? It’s like trying to distinguish yourself as real in a hyperreal tableau. You might try to strike a distinctly human pose among the other seeming humans there, but their response will be to strike one that’s even more distinctly human in its appearance even to the most refined of spectators (who have their own authentication problem as literary critics, peer reviewers, etc.).
I am of an exasperating belief yet I am not of the wisdom to — sing it with me, Xs — “hold on loosely.” Just because some misguided group of people, even half of all Americans, doesn’t believe that something exists doesn’t make it disappear. Remember climate change? Oh, right, forgive my forgetting you forgot given the faster‐hurtling extinction‐level events — unaligned AI, orange‐faced authoritarianism — on our minds.
The reality to grok is that realities themselves, not just interpretations thereof, compete today on social media. How, then, can we determine how to act in our interests if we each take a different reality for granted? Even if the government didn’t oppose our interests — like fair representation in the government — we still wouldn’t know how to act individually, never mind collectively, to further them. Our collective fucking-over would be determined even without the orangutan in office and the banana-feeders around him. As things stand, however, it’s overdetermined.
Let this be a toast to social and artistic decadence written for humans by humans, or maybe for and by machines.
The original article was published to my Substack at this link: Ecce Zoomer — by Garrett Ramos — The View from Everywhere
